commanders: Chiang Kai-shek, Zhang Zhizhong (China); Iwane Matsui (Japan)
forces: Around 700,000 Chinese troops against an eventual 300,000 Japanese troops backed by naval gunfire, armour and air support
casualties: Official Chinese reports recorded roughly 187,200 killed and wounded by early November, with later estimates reaching 300,000; Japanese losses are estimated at 60,000 or more depending on the source
outcome: Japanese victory after nearly four months of fighting; the city left devastated and China's best-trained divisions gutted
paragraphs: The Second Sino-Japanese War began on 7 July 1937 with a skirmish at the Marco Polo Bridge outside Beijing, when Japanese troops on exercise claimed to have come under fire from Chinese forces. When Chinese commander Chiang Kai-shek refused to withdraw or apologise, Japan launched a full invasion expecting a swift victory, capturing Beijing and Tianjin within three weeks. Rather than concede the north, Chiang deliberately opened a second front in Shanghai the following month, calculating that a fight in China's most cosmopolitan city, watched by foreign diplomats and journalists based in its international concessions, might draw Western sympathy and buy time for Chinese industry to relocate inland, even though historians remain divided on how much this strategy actually achieved.

Chiang committed his best-equipped forces, including the German-trained and German-armed 87th and 88th Divisions, into a city built for commerce rather than combat. What followed became one of the longest and bloodiest urban battles fought anywhere up to that point in the century; historian Peter Harmsen has described it as a preview of the kind of total urban warfare that would define the rest of the twentieth century, and by some counts it stands as the largest city battle prior to Stalingrad five years later. Fighting raged street by street through the Zhabei and Jiangwan districts, with the Chinese struggling to dislodge entrenched Japanese positions protected by naval gunfire from the Yangtze, while Japan's amphibious landings at Hangzhou Bay in early November finally turned the Chinese flank and forced a general withdrawal.

The single most famous episode of the siege came at its very end, when a battalion of around 400 men under Colonel Xie Jinyuan held the five-storey Sihang Warehouse against repeated Japanese assaults for four days in full view of tens of thousands of spectators across Suzhou Creek, becoming known as the 'Eight Hundred Heroes' even though the true number was far smaller. Prince Takamatsu, brother of Emperor Hirohito, reportedly remarked of the wider campaign that Japan intended to 'smash China so that it will be ten years before it can stand straight again,' a comment that captured the scale of ambition behind an invasion that had been expected to take months rather than years.

The cost to China was severe and lasting. Chiang lost an estimated 10,000 of the roughly 25,000 junior officers trained at the Whampoa Military Academy, and his elite German-trained divisions were reduced to a fraction of their original strength, damage from which his Central Army never fully recovered. The remaining Chinese forces withdrew toward the capital at Nanjing, pursued by a Japanese army that had been shocked by the cost of taking Shanghai and was determined not to be delayed again.
significance: The battle destroyed the core of China's modernised military just as the war was beginning, and the ferocity and frustration Japanese troops carried away from nearly four months of unexpectedly costly fighting is widely cited by historians as a direct contributing factor to the atrocities that followed weeks later at Nanjing.

commanders: Tang Shengzhi (China); Iwane Matsui, Prince Yasuhiko Asaka (Japan)
forces: Around 81,500 Chinese defenders, many untrained conscripts or exhausted veterans of Shanghai, against the advancing Japanese Central China Area Army
casualties: Estimates vary widely by source and scope, ranging from roughly 40,000 to over 300,000 Chinese deaths, with the International Military Tribunal for the Far East settling on a figure of more than 200,000
outcome: Japanese capture of the city on 13 December 1937, followed by a mass atrocity that continued for weeks
paragraphs: With Shanghai lost, China's government relocated inland, leaving General Tang Shengzhi to defend Nanjing with a hastily assembled garrison of untrained conscripts and units still battered from the Shanghai campaign. A group of foreign residents led by German businessman John Rabe established the Nanking Safety Zone, a designated area intended to shelter civilians from the fighting, while Tang publicly declared the city would be defended to the last regardless of the odds.

The city fell after four days of intense fighting on 13 December, but the collapse of Chinese command on the night of the 12th turned an organised retreat into chaos, with many soldiers stripping their uniforms in a desperate attempt to blend into the civilian population. Days earlier, Prince Yasuhiko Asaka, a member of the imperial family newly installed to command the assault, had been told by his division commanders that roughly 300,000 Chinese troops were nearly encircled and that surrender negotiations seemed likely. Asaka issued an order to 'kill all captives,' providing what amounted to official sanction for the killing that followed, both during the battle and for weeks after the city had already fallen.

For the following six weeks, Japanese troops carried out organised mass executions of captured soldiers and men of military age along the banks of the Yangtze, alongside widespread sexual violence against women and girls of all ages, and arson that destroyed roughly a third of the city. Rabe's Safety Zone, along with the efforts of missionaries such as Minnie Vautrin, is credited with sheltering some 200,000 or more civilians who would otherwise have had nowhere to hide, even as Japanese soldiers periodically entered the zone regardless. As historian Jonathan Spence later reflected, Japanese troops who had expected an easy war instead found themselves bored, exhausted, and frustrated after months of harder fighting than anticipated, conditions under which, in his words, the Chinese population 'seemed marked out as victims' regardless of age or sex.

The brutality was not simply spontaneous. Japanese military culture treated surrender as shameful, discipline within the ranks was maintained through harsh punishment that bred resentment, and years of propaganda had reinforced a belief in Chinese racial inferiority. Combined with the unexpectedly high casualties suffered at Shanghai, these pressures produced an outlet for anger that fell entirely on Nanjing's civilian population once organised resistance had collapsed.

In the aftermath, General Matsui privately expressed shame and regret over what his troops had done, reportedly weeping in front of his officers, though he took no effective action to stop the killing while it was happening. He was tried after the war by the International Military Tribunal for the Far East and executed in 1948 for failing to prevent the atrocity; Prince Asaka, protected by his status as a member of the imperial family, was granted immunity and never faced trial. The League of Nations formally condemned the killings but imposed no sanctions, a response echoed by Britain and the United States despite their own citizens witnessing the aftermath firsthand.
significance: The exact death toll remains contested to this day, with estimates ranging from tens of thousands to over 300,000 depending on the scope and timeframe used, and the massacre continues to shape Sino-Japanese relations, resurfacing as a point of diplomatic tension whenever Japanese politicians or textbooks are seen to minimise or deny what took place.

commanders: Edward Rydz-Śmigły (Poland); Fedor von Bock and Gerd von Rundstedt (Germany)
forces: Around 1 million Polish troops against roughly 1.8 million German troops, backed by overwhelming superiority in tanks and aircraft
casualties: Approximately 66,000 Polish military deaths and 660,000 taken prisoner between German and Soviet forces; German losses around 16,000 killed
outcome: German-Soviet victory within five weeks; Poland partitioned between the two powers
paragraphs: Tension between Germany and Poland had been building since the creation of the Polish state after the First World War, when the Polish Corridor was carved out of German territory to give the new nation access to the Baltic Sea, physically separating East Prussia from the rest of Germany. Danzig, a majority-German port city within this corridor, was placed under League of Nations administration rather than direct German or Polish control, and throughout 1939 Hitler repeatedly demanded its return alongside a route through the corridor, demands Poland consistently refused.

With negotiations broken down and the Nazi-Soviet Pact removing any risk of Soviet intervention, German intelligence staged a series of fabricated incidents along the border to manufacture a pretext for war, the best known being an attack on a radio station at Gleiwitz carried out by German operatives disguised as Polish soldiers. At 4:45am on 1 September, the German battleship Schleswig-Holstein opened fire on the Polish garrison at Westerplatte in Danzig, while German forces crossed the border on three fronts, converging on Warsaw from the west, north, and south.

Poland's defensive plan called for holding the western border rather than falling back to natural river barriers, a decision driven largely by fear that any retreat would invite Britain and France to negotiate a separate peace with Germany, as had happened over Czechoslovakia the previous year. That forward positioning left Polish forces stretched thin and vulnerable to the encircling German advance, and despite a determined counterattack at the Battle of the Bzura in mid-September, the largest engagement of the campaign, Polish forces were unable to regain the initiative.

On 17 September, with Polish defences already collapsing in the west, the Soviet Union invaded from the east under the secret terms of the Nazi-Soviet Pact, rendering Poland's remaining plan to hold a defensive pocket near the Romanian border obsolete overnight. Warsaw, under siege since 13 September and subjected to sustained aerial bombardment that killed thousands of civilians, held out until 28 September; the last organised Polish resistance ended on 6 October.

The invasion was accompanied by widespread atrocities against Polish civilians, including mass executions, the killing of tens of thousands of members of the Polish intelligentsia in a deliberate campaign to eliminate the country's political and cultural leadership, and the beginning of what historians describe as the opening phase of the Holocaust in its broader sense. Poland never formally surrendered, and thousands of its soldiers and much of its navy escaped to continue fighting alongside Britain and France for the remainder of the war.
significance: The invasion of Poland is universally recognised as the formal start of the Second World War in Europe, and American journalist John Gunther's contemporary verdict that 'the German campaign was a masterpiece' reflected how thoroughly it demonstrated the coordinated, fast-moving warfare that would define much of the conflict that followed.

commanders: Maurice Gamelin, later Maxime Weygand, and Lord Gort (Allies); Gerd von Rundstedt and Fedor von Bock (Germany)
forces: Roughly 3.3 million Allied troops, 4,000 tanks and 2,900 aircraft against 3.35 million German troops, 2,445 tanks and 5,638 aircraft
casualties: Over 73,000 Allied troops killed and 1.75 million captured, the overwhelming majority French; 338,000 Allied troops evacuated from Dunkirk
outcome: German victory; France surrendered on 22 June 1940 after a six-week campaign
paragraphs: British, French and Belgian forces spent the months before the invasion in defensive positions along France's borders, placing their faith heavily in the Maginot Line, a heavily fortified defensive network on which France had spent some 3 billion francs during the interwar years. Allied tactical thinking had changed little since the First World War, and commanders broadly expected 1940 to bring the same kind of attritional, trench-based warfare that had defined the previous conflict an assumption that would prove disastrously mistaken. General Philippe Pétain had declared the Ardennes 'impenetrable,' and French commander-in-chief Maurice Gamelin agreed, noting it 'never favoured large operations,' a view reinforced by French war games in 1938 that left commanders convinced no major armoured thrust could emerge from that terrain.

Germany's plan, codenamed Case Yellow, was designed to exploit exactly that assumption. The plan had evolved significantly from its original form early drafts called for a frontal advance through Belgium, but after the Mechelen Incident in January 1940, when German aircraft carrying Luftwaffe plans force-landed in Belgium and the documents were captured, the plan was fundamentally revised. The final version, shaped decisively by the thinking of General Erich von Manstein and armoured warfare theorist Heinz Guderian, called for a diversionary attack through Belgium and the Netherlands to draw Allied forces north, while the true weight of the offensive seven panzer divisions would drive through the Ardennes Forest, terrain the Allies had left comparatively lightly defended and considered impassable for armour.

German paratroopers landed in Belgium and the Netherlands on 10 May, and Allied commanders responded exactly as German planners had hoped, committing their best and most mobile forces north into Belgium under Plan D to meet the threat, while maintaining strong positions at the Maginot Line. On 13 May, Panzergruppe Kleist forced three crossings of the Meuse near Sedan, supported by the heaviest aerial bombardment the world had yet witnessed nearly 4,000 Luftwaffe sorties in a single day broke French defensive lines and shattered the morale of reserve divisions holding the centre. The 'Panic of Bulson' that evening saw French artillerymen abandon their positions before a single German tank had crossed, opening a gap the panzers immediately exploited.

By 15 to 21 May, German Blitzkrieg tactics had shattered the major Allied defensive lines entirely, with Guderian's panzers racing westward down the Somme valley toward the English Channel at speeds that alarmed even Hitler, who temporarily ordered a halt for fear the advance was moving too fast to be sustained. On 20 May, German units reached the coast near Abbeville, cutting off the Allied forces in Belgium and northern France roughly 400,000 men of the British Expeditionary Force, the French First Army, and the Belgian Army from the rest of France entirely.

By 24 May, those trapped forces had been pushed back to the coast around Dunkirk. In one of the most debated decisions of the war, Hitler ordered his panzer divisions to halt outside the town, persuaded partly by Hermann Göring's assurance that the Luftwaffe alone could finish off the trapped army from the air, and partly by concerns about the terrain and the need to conserve armour for the coming offensive southward against the remaining French forces. That pause gave Britain a window it would not otherwise have had, and the subsequent evacuation Operation Dynamo rescued 338,000 Allied troops between 26 May and 4 June, far exceeding the 20,000 to 30,000 planners had hoped to save. The scale of the evacuation, and the role played by hundreds of civilian vessels alongside Royal Navy ships, became one of the defining episodes of Britain's war.

Even with those men rescued, France was left to defend itself with a badly weakened force. Germany launched Case Red on 5 June, driving southward against the reconstituted French line on the Somme and Aisne. Despite a more determined French resistance than the opening phase had seen French tactics had improved, morale had partially recovered, and some armoured divisions had been rebuilt the German combination of air superiority and armoured mobility proved decisive again. Paris fell on 14 June. The French government surrendered on 22 June 1940, and organised fighting officially ended on 25 June, bringing a campaign that had lasted six weeks to a close. Winston Churchill, while careful to praise the Dunkirk evacuation, was blunt about the wider defeat, calling it a 'colossal military disaster' and cautioning that 'wars are not won by evacuations.'
significance: The campaign left Britain standing alone against a Germany that had overrun Poland, Luxembourg, Belgium, the Netherlands and France in under a year, and demonstrated so thoroughly the obsolescence of Allied tactical thinking that it forced a fundamental reappraisal of armoured warfare across every major military. Hitler's attention turned next to knocking Britain out of the war entirely a task that would prove far harder than the six weeks it had taken to defeat France.

commanders: Lord Gort (British Expeditionary Force); Jean Abrial (French naval commander); Bertram Ramsay (Royal Navy, Operation Dynamo); Gerd von Rundstedt and Fedor von Bock (Germany)
forces: Approximately 400,000 Allied troops trapped; evacuated by a fleet of 861 vessels including warships, merchant ships, and hundreds of civilian craft
casualties: 338,226 Allied troops evacuated; roughly 16,000 French and 1,000 British soldiers killed during the battle and evacuation; nearly all heavy equipment, vehicles, artillery and tanks abandoned on the beaches
outcome: Successful evacuation of the bulk of the trapped Allied army, though France was left to continue fighting without them and the materiel losses were enormous
paragraphs: By 24 May 1940, roughly 400,000 Allied troops the British Expeditionary Force, the French First Army, and the Belgian Army had been cut off from the rest of France and pushed back toward the Channel coast around Dunkirk. German forces had reached the sea at Abbeville four days earlier, completing an encirclement that left the trapped armies with their backs to the water and seemingly no way out. BEF commander General Lord Gort wrote privately to Anthony Eden that a great part of his force and its equipment would 'inevitably be lost in the best of circumstances,' and American journalist William Shirer reported from German military circles that the fate of the Allied army was considered sealed.

In one of the most debated decisions of the entire war, Hitler issued the Halt Order on 24 May, stopping his panzer divisions outside Dunkirk for three days. The order did not originate with Hitler alone Generals von Rundstedt and von Kluge had recommended halting to consolidate, and Hitler sanctioned it with the support of the Wehrmacht high command. Luftwaffe commander Hermann Göring seized the opportunity to claim the glory of finishing off the trapped army from the air, assuring Hitler that his aircraft could complete the job without the panzers. The true reason for the halt remains debated: some historians point to concerns about the boggy Flemish terrain unsuitable for armour, others to the need to conserve tanks for the coming offensive southward against the remaining French forces. Hitler did not rescind the order until the evening of 26 May three days that proved decisive.

Recognising the scale of the unfolding disaster, Royal Navy Vice-Admiral Bertram Ramsay organised Operation Dynamo from his headquarters beneath Dover Castle, drawing together over 800 vessels between 26 May and 4 June. The docks at Dunkirk were too badly damaged to use, so Captain William Tennant made the crucial decision to load troops directly from the East Mole, a concrete sea wall not designed for the purpose, which dramatically increased the numbers that could be embarked each day. On 31 May alone, more than 68,000 men were lifted. Many of the vessels were not naval ships at all but civilian 'little ships' fishing boats, pleasure craft, Thames river ferries and lifeboats crewed by volunteers who navigated the Channel under Luftwaffe attack to reach the beaches. The RAF committed every available fighter to cover the evacuation from the air, flying continuously to keep German bombers away from the ships, though at a cost of 177 aircraft destroyed or damaged.

The fighting on the ground was desperate throughout. The Belgian army surrendered on 28 May, opening a 20-mile gap in the eastern flank that Gort was forced to plug with exhausted divisions. French forces under General Molinié fought a remarkable rearguard action at Lille, with five encircled divisions holding out for four days against seven German divisions, a sacrifice that historians credit with keeping those German forces away from Dunkirk and allowing an estimated 100,000 additional Allied troops to escape. On the perimeter itself, French and British troops endured sustained artillery bombardment and Luftwaffe strafing, holding the line while the evacuation continued behind them. Those who held the final rearguard positions were among the last to leave, and tens of thousands of French soldiers who had protected the escape were themselves taken prisoner when the perimeter finally collapsed on 4 June.

Where planners had hoped to save perhaps 20,000 to 30,000 men, the final total reached 338,226, including roughly 200,000 British and 139,000 French, Belgian, Dutch and Polish troops. But the material cost was staggering. The British Army left behind 880 field guns, around 500 anti-aircraft guns, 850 anti-tank guns, nearly 700 tanks, 11,000 machine guns, 45,000 vehicles, and enough equipment to fit out eight to ten divisions. Army equipment available in Britain after Dunkirk was sufficient to arm barely two divisions, and the shortage was so severe that the Royal Army Service Corps resorted to retrieving obsolete buses from scrapyards to use as troop transports some still in service as late as the North African campaign of 1942.

Churchill was careful from the outset not to allow the relief of the rescue to obscure the reality of what had happened. Addressing the House of Commons on 4 June, he praised the evacuation and those who made it possible, but was direct about the wider picture: 'Wars are not won by evacuations.' On 2 June, the Dean of St Paul's had been the first to call it the 'Miracle of Dunkirk,' and the term entered the language immediately newspapers filled with letters from readers connecting the calm seas and the mist that hampered German bombers to divine intervention, and the image of ordinary civilians sailing into a war zone became a defining story of British national character.
significance: Dunkirk preserved the trained core of Britain's army at the exact moment when invasion seemed most imminent, and without it Britain would have faced the summer of 1940 with almost no land forces capable of resisting a German landing. The civilian rescue effort became a foundational myth of British wartime identity the 'Dunkirk Spirit' of solidarity in adversity even as Churchill himself insisted it be understood as a deliverance from disaster rather than any kind of victory.
